picks.title: “We strongly believe that in the coming years, writing code manually won’t be a thing”: Inside the new wave of AI vibe coding tools turning prompts into plugins
picks.excerpt: AI-powered plugin generators like Amorph and Pluginmaker.ai let musicians build custom audio plugins from natural-language prompts instead of traditional coding, pointing to a new 'vibe coding' workflow for music-tool creation.
picks.rationale: Vibe-coding plugin generators are a brand-new making-it workflow that changes how producers build their own tools tomorrow.

picks.title: AI Music Deals: Musicians' Note Warns to Not Override Artists' Rights
picks.excerpt: A global coalition of musicians, songwriters and managers released a letter warning record labels and publishers against coercing artists into AI licensing deals, arguing creators deserve the same training-consent rights the labels demand of AI firms.
picks.rationale: A global artist coalition setting terms on AI licensing reframes the consent debate every creator now has to navigate.

picks.title: Two Notes Audio Engineering releases Genome Intro, a FREE amp and FX suite
picks.excerpt: Two Notes' free Genome Intro is powered by CODEX, an AI amp playback engine that loads and plays neural amp captures from Neural Amp Modeller, AIDA-X and Proteus inside one workspace.
picks.rationale: A free AI-driven amp/FX suite that loads NAM, AIDA-X and Proteus captures is an immediately usable tool drop for producers.

picks.title: Apple Intros Music Understanding Framework At WWDC
picks.excerpt: Apple's new on-device Music Understanding framework, unveiled at WWDC 2026, uses AI audio analysis to extract time-stamped data across six musical dimensions — rhythm, key, loudness, pace, structure and instrument activity — for app developers.
picks.rationale: An on-device framework exposing rhythm, key and structure data opens a new platform for music-app builders to act on.

picks.title: Was your music used to train AI? This free tool will tell you
picks.excerpt: A free searchable database built by The Atlantic lets musicians check whether their recordings appear in four major AI music training datasets, revealing millions of tracks potentially used without explicit consent.
picks.rationale: A free database to check if your tracks are in AI training sets is a concrete action any artist can take right now.
